review_summary: Transcript is sufficient for reinforcement questions about polysemy, multi-sense embeddings, single-vector averaging, and sparse recovery ideas. The examples and contrasts are explained verbally, so no separate clip is needed.

question_bank.question: What is the main idea behind multi-prototype or multi-sense embeddings?
choices: Assign every word exactly one fixed global meaning
Cluster occurrences of a word by context and learn a vector for each sense cluster
Replace word vectors with character n-grams only
Train separate vocabularies for nouns and verbs
question_bank.explanation: Multi-sense embeddings try to separate a word's usages by grouping tokens with similar contexts, then learning a representation for each group.

question_bank.question: Why do many modern systems still keep one vector per word despite polysemy?
choices: Because words never actually have multiple meanings
Because dictionaries agree perfectly on sense boundaries
Because many sense boundaries are fuzzy, and one vector can be viewed as an average over contexts
Because single-vector models require no training data
question_bank.explanation: Many meanings blend into one another rather than forming clean discrete categories, so a single word vector is often treated as an average over the contexts in which the word appears.
